phase1.pulse_reasoning: Expansion includes weather forecasting variants (forecast vs forecasting, American vs British spellings), cooling infrastructure terms (center/centre, shelter, refuge), temperature-health relationship terminology, methodological terms essential for causal inference studies (randomized, quasi-experimental, natural experiment), and health outcome measures. Keywords capture both the intervention mechanism (weather forecasts enabling cooling center deployment) and outcome measurement (temperature-health effects).

phase1.formulator_reasoning: The search strategy uses a five-concept AND structure: (1) weather forecasting terms, (2) cooling/heat intervention facilities, (3) temperature-health relationship terms, (4) rigorous research design methods, and (5) health outcome measures. This approach ensures we capture studies that evaluate cooling interventions triggered by weather forecasts using causal inference methods. The Elsevier query uses wildcards for plurals and variations. The PubMed query explicitly uses [Title/Abstract] tags and includes both US/UK spelling variants. The OpenAlex query is simplified but maintains the core concept structure. The methodological filter is crucial to focus on studies with plausible causal identification rather than purely observational work.

Added broader forecasting terms (early warning, heat warning, prediction), more diverse cooling intervention terms (public cooling, community cooling, heat relief, cooling services), additional health exposure terms (thermal stress, heat/temperature exposure), more causal inference methods (difference-in-difference, regression discontinuity, instrumental variables), and expanded health outcome measures. issues_resolved: Expanded forecasting terminology to reduce restrictiveness and capture studies using different forecast-related terms

Enhanced health outcome coverage to capture various healthcare utilization measures
Maintained OpenAlex compatibility while preserving comprehensive search coverage

phase1.formulator_reasoning: Query structure uses four concept groups connected by AND: (1) Weather forecasting/prediction systems, (2) Cooling interventions/facilities, (3) Health outcomes (temperature-related mortality/morbidity), and (4) Rigorous study designs for causal inference. Each concept group uses OR to capture synonyms and variations. Used wildcards strategically in Elsevier (forecast*, cent*, program*) to capture plural forms and variations. PubMed version includes both 'program' and 'programme' spelling variants. OpenAlex version is simplified without wildcards as the platform handles variations internally. The methodological filter is comprehensive to capture various causal identification strategies beyond just RCTs, which is crucial for policy intervention studies where randomization may be rare. This structure balances sensitivity (capturing relevant studies across different terminologies) with specificity (requiring all four key concepts to be present).

phase1.refiner_notes: Enhanced all queries to address limited results warning by expanding synonym coverage across all concept groups. Added MeSH terms to PubMed query for biomedical focus. Included additional forecasting terms (climate, seasonal, extreme heat), expanded cooling facility terminology (community, emergency, refuge, respite), broadened health outcome terms (heat-related deaths, thermal stress, excess mortality), and strengthened methodological terms (impact evaluation, treatment effects, counterfactual). All syntax verified and queries are execution-ready.
issues_resolved: Added broader synonym coverage to increase retrieval volumes while maintaining precision
Integrated relevant MeSH terms and Publication Types in PubMed query for enhanced biomedical literature capture
Expanded forecasting terminology to include climate and seasonal forecasting
Added community-based and emergency cooling facility terms
Strengthened health outcome terminology with heat-specific mortality and morbidity terms
Enhanced causal methodology terms with impact evaluation and treatment effect terminology
